Figure 8: METAGAME quantifies gradient-based token interactions in vision-language encoders.

Table 2: Interaction recognition on ImageNet-1k. Our proposed METAGAME correctly attributes cross-modal interactions in vision–language encoders of various architectures and sizes. It improves the performance of first-order explanation methods based on attention, representations, and gradients. The second-order, black-box FIxLIP baseline in gray takes orders of magnitude longer to compute. Extended results for SigLIP-2 (ViT-L/16) and SigLIP-2 (ViT-B/32) are in Appendix, Table 4.
Figure 4: METAGAME quantifies concept interactions in multimodal diffusion transformers.

Table 1: Analytical interactions φj→i for f(x) = x1+I , where I := x1x 2 2. Unlike serial approaches, meta-attributions perfectly isolate the individual effect x1 (φi→i) and the interaction I (φj→i). Furthermore, whereas set-based interactions collapse effects into undirected subsets, meta-attributions capture the directional hierarchy and sum to the first-order attribution ϕi.

Table 4: Interaction recognition on ImageNet-1k. Extended Table 2. Our proposed METAGAME correctly attributes cross-modal interactions in vision–language encoders, improving the performance of first-order explanation methods based on attention and gradients. The second-order, black-box FIxLIP baseline in gray takes orders of magnitude longer to compute, depending on hyperparameters.
Figure 13: Ablations extending Table 3. METAGAME improves ConceptAttention for the FLUX.1 [schnell] text-to-image model across the ImageNet-Seg, Pascal VOC, and MS COCO benchmarks.
Figure 7: Example of Meta-Grad-ECLIP explaining MetaCLIP-2 on a single input from the pointing game, which is used to measure the interaction recognition evaluation metric.
